title: Supply Crate Inspection
desc: A surface shipment of paper, cloth, and wax has arrived. The humidity wants it first.

steps.text:
Surface shipments do not arrive often at the embassy, and when they do everyone becomes briefly alert in the manner of creatures watching fragile wealth descend into hostile weather. Today's crate train contains writing paper, sealing wax, lamp oil, curtain cloth, and two boxes of tea wrapped as if the courier expected Mount Pillar itself to take offense at the smell.

The crates have only been inside for twenty minutes and one already feels damp along the bottom seam. The receiving clerk, who distrusts both moisture and haste, looks from the crates to the walls as if calculating which of them is more likely to ruin her day. "Paper first if you value treaties," she says. "Cloth first if you value not scrubbing mildew out of curtains before they've even been hung."

She hands you a pry bar and the inventory tablet. "Open the paper crate and inspect for edge damp, or sort the cloth and wax before the room decides they belong to it."

options.label: Open the paper crate and check the documents stock
Sort the cloth, wax, and lamp supplies first

steps.text:
Paper is deceptive cargo. It looks tidy, dry, and manageable right up until the edges curl and the whole stack becomes an expensive lesson in underground storage. You pry the crate open, separate the wrapped bundles, and test each pack by touch. The outer layers of two bundles have taken on a faint softness from damp creeping through the seam.

The receiving clerk wastes no sympathy on them. "Drying rack," she says at once. The affected sheets are spread flat on slatted stone under the cleanest vent while the untouched bundles go straight into sealed cabinets with fresh desiccant jars. You rewrap the rack-dried stock once the curl relaxes and mark the crate seam for repair before the next shipment arrives.

When the count is finished, the loss is minor - a handful of spoiled cover sheets, nothing more. The clerk records it with visible satisfaction. "Acceptable damage," she says, which in her voice sounds as close to happiness as paperwork ever gets. The embassy keeps its paper. In a place like this, that counts as a logistical victory.

options.label: Seal the inventory and stack the good bundles away

steps.text:
The cloth bolts are wrapped in waxed hide, the sealing sticks bundled in straw, and the lamp oil packed in ceramic jars cushioned with shredded reed. None of it likes damp. You open each crate in turn, checking for soft spots, mildew scent, or wax bloom from temperature shift.

One cloth roll has picked up moisture along the outer layer. You cut the binding, peel away the affected section, and hang the good length over a drying frame before the mold can advance. The sealing wax fares better, though two sticks have fused together in the bundle and need to be separated with a warmed knife. The lamp oil jars are intact, which the clerk notes with the solemn gratitude usually reserved for diplomatic miracles.

By the time you finish, the receiving table looks orderly again: dry cloth stacked by weight, wax counted and boxed, oil set aside for the evening lamps. The clerk checks your tally, then the stacks, then the repaired crate labels. "Good," she says. "Nothing here has become a cleaning task yet. That puts us ahead of schedule."

options.label: Finish the inventory before the room grows damp again
